common_name: Shadow Witch Orchid (Racemose Ponthieva, Wild Orchid, Moccasin Flower)

planting.note: Plant in fall in partial to full shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral woodland soil; Ponthieva racemosa grows naturally in moist, shaded, calcareous woodland soils throughout the southeastern US and is one of the few native orchids that can be successfully grown in woodland garden conditions. The basal leaf rosette overwinters in zone 8-10; the plant blooms in fall and winter. Best established from small plants or divisions from reputable nurseries propagating this native species legally — never collect from the wild. Non-toxic.

directions: Plant in partial to full shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained woodland soil — Shadow Witch Orchid grows naturally in the deep, moist, humus-rich soils of shaded floodplain forests, seepage slopes, and limestone woodland margins throughout the southeastern US and requires cool, moist shade conditions for successful establishment. Ponthieva racemosa is a deciduous terrestrial orchid with a wide natural range from Virginia and Florida south through the Caribbean, Mexico, Central America, and into South America in the Orchidaceae family. The plant produces a flat, ground-level rosette of 3–5 broadly elliptic to oval, dark-green, slightly fleshy basal leaves that overwinter and persist until spring in the southern part of the range. In fall and early winter, an erect, somewhat glandular-hairy flower spike 10–20 inches tall emerges bearing 10–25 small (0.5-inch) flowers in white to greenish-white with the two upper petals distinctively patterned with green veins and spots — the petals held in an unusual, somewhat spreading, wing-like position that gave rise to the poetic common name 'Shadow Witch.' The flowers are self-fertilizing in many populations, ensuring seed set without pollinators. One of the more shade-tolerant and cold-hardy of the southeastern native orchids, making it suitable for woodland garden cultivation in zone 8-10 with appropriate moist, shaded conditions. Non-toxic.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Ponthieva racemosa: Shadow Witch native orchid
Florida Native Plant Society — Ponthieva racemosa species profile
North American Native Orchid Alliance — Ponthieva racemosa cultivation notes
Florida Department of Environmental Protection — Native Orchids of Florida

common_name: Antenna Orchid (Trigger Orchid, Scorpion Orchid, Antennifera Restrepia)

planting.note: Grow in bright indirect to filtered light in small pots of fine bark or sphagnum moss-bark mix in a cool to intermediate location; Restrepia antennifera is a cool-growing orchid from high-altitude Colombian cloud forests (5,000–9,000 feet) that prefers cooler temperatures (day 65–75°F, night 50–60°F). Excellent terrarium orchid — the small size and preference for high humidity (70%+) make it ideal for enclosed humid conditions. The leaves produce plantlets (keikis) at the leaf tips that can be detached and rooted. Blooms sequentially through most of the year. Non-toxic.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to filtered light in cool, humid conditions — Antenna Orchid is a miniature cloud forest orchid that grows most happily in the cool, high-humidity conditions of a terrarium or cool shaded porch and will decline in hot, dry summer conditions. Restrepia antennifera is a tiny, sympodial, epiphytic orchid native to the high-altitude cloud forests of Colombia and Venezuela in the Orchidaceae family, one of the most popular miniature orchids for terrarium culture for the extraordinary appearance of its flowers. The plant is very small — barely 4–8 inches tall — with a single, broadly oval, thick, leathery leaf per stem. But the flowers produced from the leaf base are visually extraordinary: the two lateral sepals are broad, fused along their inner margins into a single, deflexed 'synsepal' structure in yellow-orange to orange-red with contrasting purple-red veining and spotting — forming the lower visible portion of the flower. The two upper petals are highly modified into extremely long, thin, thread-like structures terminating in slightly thickened, warty, club-shaped tips — the 'antennae' that give the plant its common name and that wave gently in air movement. The lip is small and attached to the moveable column in a trigger mechanism related to pollinator deception. Restrepia antennifera produces flowers sequentially from the same leaf nodes, providing months of bloom from a small plant. An outstanding miniature orchid for terrarium culture. Non-toxic.
sources: American Orchid Society — Restrepia antennifera cultivation guide
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Restrepia antennifera species profile
International Orchid Conservation — Restrepia: cloud forest miniature orchids
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Restrepia (non-toxic to pets)

common_name: Goblin Orchid (Trumpet Mormodes, Twisted-Column Orchid, Bugle Orchid)

planting.note: Grow in an open basket with coarse bark in bright indirect to partial direct light; Mormodes buccinator is a deciduous orchid — it sheds its leaves in fall as the pseudobulbs ripen and blooms from the leafless pseudobulbs in winter on pendant to arching spikes. A dry rest period in fall (reduce watering as leaves yellow) is important for triggering bloom. Resume watering as new growth starts in spring. The twisted, asymmetric flowers are pollinated by male orchid bees that are thrown off the trigger mechanism onto the bee's body in different positions depending on flower orientation, ensuring cross-pollination. Non-toxic.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to partial direct morning light in coarse bark medium in an open basket — Goblin Orchid grows most vigorously in good light with the open-basket culture that provides the root aeration appropriate to its natural epiphytic habitat in tropical deciduous forests. Mormodes buccinator is a deciduous, sympodial, epiphytic orchid native to Mexico, Guatemala, Belize, Honduras, El Salvador, Nicaragua, and Costa Rica in the Orchidaceae family, one of the most scientifically fascinating of the New World orchids because of its uniquely asymmetric, twisted flowers and sophisticated pollination mechanism. The plant produces large, fusiform (spindle-shaped) pseudobulbs with several broad, pleated leaves that drop in fall. From the leafless pseudobulbs in winter, pendant to arching flower spikes emerge bearing 8–20 medium-sized flowers (1.5–2 inches) that are immediately remarkable for their twisted, asymmetric form: the column is rotated (twisted) to the right or left relative to the flower center, with the lip pointing to one side rather than forward or downward as in most orchids. This asymmetric column orientation — with the trigger mechanism in a different position in different flowers or between different plants — is related to the pollination system: male euglossine bees (orchid bees) that land on the trigger are catapulted by the spring-loaded mechanism onto their dorsal (right) or ventral (left) surface, depending on flower orientation, ensuring that pollinia from differently-oriented flowers are placed on different body positions of the bee — a mechanism designed to prevent self-pollination. Flowers vary from orange-yellow to brown-maroon. Non-toxic.
sources: American Orchid Society — Mormodes buccinator: Goblin Orchid cultivation guide
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Mormodes species: twisted-column orchids
Dodson, C.H. — Pollination of Mormodes by euglossine bees (Annals of the Missouri Botanical Garden)
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Mormodes (non-toxic to pets)

common_name: Gold Lycaste (Dowiana Lycaste, Costa Rica Lycaste, Golden-Lipped Orchid)

planting.note: Grow in bright indirect to filtered direct light in bark-based medium in a clay or wood container; Lycaste dowiana requires a cool-dry winter rest (nights 50–60°F, reduced watering October–February) for spring blooming. Without cool nights, the plant vegetates but does not flower. The leaves drop in fall as the plant enters dormancy. Flowers emerge from leafless or new-growth pseudobulbs in late winter or spring. One of the most beautiful yellow-flowered Lycaste species, similar in culture to L. aromatica. Non-toxic to pets.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to filtered direct morning light in bark medium in a clay or wooden pot — Gold Lycaste performs best in cool, well-ventilated conditions and benefits from the temperature differential between day and night typical of intermediate orchid cultivation. Lycaste dowiana is a deciduous, sympodial, epiphytic orchid native to Costa Rica and Panama in the Orchidaceae family, one of the most ornamentally beautiful of the Lycaste species and particularly valued for its large, richly colored flowers in golden-yellow. The plant produces large, angular pseudobulbs with broad, pleated leaves (up to 24 inches long) that drop in fall as the plant prepares for its winter rest period. From the leafless pseudobulbs in late winter and spring, individual flower spikes emerge (multiple per pseudobulb on well-established plants), each carrying a single large (4–5 inch), long-lasting flower in brilliant golden-yellow with an intensely colored, orange-red to orange-maroon, three-lobed lip that provides a vivid color contrast against the warm yellow petals. The flowers are pleasantly fragrant, with a sweet, honey-like quality. As with most Lycaste species, L. dowiana has contributed to many of the large-flowered hybrid Lycaste and Lycaste alliance orchids popular in cultivation, contributing its yellow color and vigor to the hybrids. An elegant, free-flowering orchid for zone 9-11 collectors with cool winter conditions. Non-toxic.
sources: American Orchid Society — Lycaste dowiana cultivation guide
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Lycaste dowiana species profile
Royal Horticultural Society — Lycaste cultivation notes
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Lycaste (non-toxic to pets)

common_name: Dinema (Twin Spur Orchid, Polybulbon Epidendrum, Caribbean Creeper)

planting.note: Grow mounted on bark or cork, or in a shallow basket with coarse bark; Dinema polybulbon is an excellent beginner orchid for outdoor culture in zone 9-11 — it is cold-tolerant, fast-growing, and produces its modest but pleasant fragrant flowers in late fall and winter. The creeping, spreading rhizomes quickly colonize the surface of a cork mount or flat bark piece. Water regularly in active growth; reduce slightly in winter. Tolerates temperatures briefly to 38–40°F. Non-toxic to pets.

directions: Grow mounted on rough bark or cork in bright indirect to filtered direct morning light with moderate humidity — Dinema polybulbon is one of the most cold-tolerant small epiphytic orchids for zone 9-11 outdoor culture and adapts exceptionally well to outdoor mounted culture on a shaded, protected porch. Dinema polybulbon (formerly Epidendrum polybulbon) is a small, creeping, sympodial, epiphytic orchid native to Mexico, Guatemala, Honduras, Belize, Cuba, Jamaica, Hispaniola, and Puerto Rico in the Orchidaceae family — one of the cold-hardiest of the small, Caribbean-origin epiphytic orchids regularly available in the orchid trade. The plant spreads by creeping rhizomes that slowly colonize the surface of its mount, each section producing a very small, round to oblong pseudobulb topped with 2 narrow, stiff, dark-green leaves about 2–3 inches long. In late fall and winter, short flower spikes (2–3 inches) emerge from between the leaves, bearing 1–2 small (1-inch) flowers in yellow-white with yellow-orange stripes on the sepals and petals and a white to cream lip — modest in size but pleasantly fragrant on warm days. The plant's creeping, mat-forming growth habit, cold tolerance, and unfussy culture make it an excellent starter orchid for zone 9-11 collectors beginning outdoor epiphyte culture. Non-toxic.
sources: American Orchid Society — Dinema polybulbon (Epidendrum polybulbon) cultivation guide
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Dinema polybulbon species profile
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Cold-hardy small orchids for zone 9-10 outdoor culture
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Dinema (non-toxic to pets)
